evidence_basis_summary: Full text concerns interviewer-mode comparison, not inclusion/exclusion of materials for an analysis universe, so role is confidently not applicable.
notes: Upgraded low to high: full PDF read confirms no relevance-adjudication work is performed.

evidence_basis_summary: Paper reports a proof-of-concept mode comparison with descriptive metrics and makes no causal-mechanism claims about political attitudes, so role is confidently not applicable.
notes: Upgraded low to high based on full-text read.

evidence_basis_summary: Human authors design the politics/democracy questionnaire and write the coding manuals defining desirable/undesirable interviewer behavior and response-quality categories that frame what the interview produces.
notes: Upgraded low to high: full text shows human-defined questionnaire (Sec 3.2/3.4) and coding manuals (Sec 3.5, App G/H) set the construct frame.

evidence_basis_summary: Case compares interviewer modes on a single student-sample questionnaire; no corpus-curation question is at stake, so role is confidently not applicable.
notes: Upgraded low to high based on full-text read.

evidence_basis_summary: Authors pre-registered the study on OSF, scripted the seminar procedure, published code and data for reuse, and designed the multi-metric evaluation pipeline with real-time problem logging.
notes: Upgraded low to high; observable also upgraded partial to yes given explicit pre-registration (Sec 3), data/code release (Sec 1), and logged procedures.

evidence_basis_summary: Authors explicitly flag small-N student sample and caution about generalizing beyond the controlled setting, implying human judgment on transportability of the proof-of-concept.
notes: Upgraded low to medium; observable upgraded no to partial. Transportability caveats appear (Sec 4 interpreted with caution; Sec 5 implementation-dependent), but not a central focus.

evidence_basis_summary: Entire study is a head-to-head test of whether the human interviewer changes the data respondents produce on political topics; human and AI conditions are compared on engagement, empathy, specificity, and interestingness.
notes: Upgraded low to high: this is the case's core question (Sec 3, 4, Table 1).

evidence_basis_summary: Authors own authorship and pre-registration, obtain participant consent, assign student observers to real-time problem logging, and publish code/data under a named repository, explicitly allocating responsibility.
notes: Upgraded low to high: consent (Sec 3.1), pre-registration, named authorship, and observer role in AI condition document clear accountability allocation.

evidence_basis_summary: Human-authored coding manuals define categorical rules for admissible interviewer behavior and response quality dimensions (clarity, relevance, specificity, engagement, empathy).
notes: Upgraded low to high: Sec 3.5 and Appendices G/H show explicit boundary setting on what counts as good interviewer behavior and good response.

evidence_basis_summary: Study does not attempt to preserve subgroup disagreement or representational pluralism; it contrasts two interview modes on a homogeneous student sample.
notes: Upgraded low to high based on full-text read.

evidence_basis_summary: Authors caution that findings are a small-N proof-of-concept whose success depends on precise implementation, signaling transportability judgment without making it the focus.
notes: Upgraded low to medium; observable upgraded no to partial. Concentrated in Sec 4 caution and Sec 5 implementation-dependence recommendations.

evidence_basis_summary: Human interview condition functions as the reference benchmark against which AI interviews are calibrated on tokens-per-answer, readability, and human-rated quality dimensions.
notes: Upgraded low to medium; observable upgraded no to partial. Table 1 and Sec 3.5 show human interviews used as the comparator, which is calibration-like though not framed that way.

evidence_basis_summary: The human interviewer's presence is directly tested: human interviewers elicit higher engagement, empathy, specificity, and interestingness, while AI elicits longer but less specific responses, showing the human interviewer measurably changes disclosure.
notes: Upgraded low to high: Table 1 and Fig 3/4 directly adjudicate this role. Status remains conditional because AI achieves parity on some dimensions (clarity, relevance, overall satisfaction).

evidence_basis_summary: Human coders and observers are needed to interpret interview transcripts, flag guideline violations, and record real-time technical problems that shape what the data mean.
notes: Upgraded low to high: Sec 3.5 research-assistant coding and Sec 4 observer problem-logging give direct evidence.

evidence_basis_summary: No title/abstract triage or early ranking task exists in this interview-methodology case, so the cluster is confidently not applicable.
notes: Upgraded low to high based on full-text read.

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_basis_summary: Two human research assistants double-coded every interviewer turn and every response against detailed manuals; coding was not delegated to AI, so the shrinking-task pattern does not appear here.
notes: Upgraded low to high: Sec 3.5 explicitly keeps humans as coders for both interviewer behavior and response quality.

evidence_basis_summary: Paper describes AI as interviewer, not as writing assistant; no delegation of manuscript drafting or prose polishing to AI is reported.
notes: Upgraded low to high based on full-text read.
